FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (ABCD), n=4,641, ages 9-12 at baseline, multi-site U.S. cohort

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-lagged panel models (CLPMs) examining reciprocal relationships among sleep, SMA, and psychopathology over 3 timepoints

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 met: SMA (screen media activity) is measured as self-reported hours on non-school-related screen time including TV/streaming, YouTube, video games, texting, social networking, video chatting. Paired with individual-trait-level variables: psychopathology (externalizing, internalizing), sleep duration, and brain structural covariation (thalamus-PFC-brainstem)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: bidirectional associations with Î˛=-0.10 [95% CI: -0.16, -0.03] for SMAâ†’sleep, Î˛=-0.11 for sleepâ†’SMA, Î˛=0.07 for externalizingâ†’SMA, indirect effects Î˛=0.032 [95% CI: 0.003, 0.067]

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported for SMâ†”outcome relationships across discovery and validation samples with replication

FT_EC7_reason: Design includes bidirectional/cross-lagged relationships where SMA functions as both IV and DV; EC7 does not apply
notes: Strong evidence of phone/SM-specific variable (SMA composite) paired with multiple individual-trait outcomes; findings replicated in validation sample

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (ABCD), n=10,828, ages 9-10 at baseline, multi-site U.S. cohort

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multilevel logistic regression with bidirectional pathways examining associations between baseline behaviors and follow-up symptoms, and reverse directionality

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 met: Screen time measured as self-reported hours/day on six screen types (viewing/streaming TV shows/movies, YouTube, video games, texting, video chatting, social networking sites) separately for weekdays and weekend days. Paired with individual-trait-level outcome: internalizing symptoms (withdrawn/depressed, anxious/depressed, somatic complaints subscales)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: adjusted OR 0.6 [95% CI 0.4-0.8] for sleep duration on withdrawn/depressed symptoms; adjusted OR 1.6 [95% CI 1.1-2.2] for weekend screen time on withdrawn/depressed symptoms in females

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results for SMâ†”outcome relationships reported in multiple models (unadjusted and adjusted) with sensitivity analyses

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional relationships tested: reverse causality models examined with internalizing symptoms as predictor of future screen time and sleep; screen time functions as both IV and DV
notes: Clear phone/SM-specific exposure paired with individual-trait mental health outcome; findings gender-stratified and behavior-specific

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (ABCD), n=10,544, ages 9-11 at baseline, multi-site U.S. cohort, nationally representative

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: isotemporal substitution and compositional data analysis (CoDA) models examining longitudinal associations between screen time forms and BMI

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 met: Screen time measured separately by form (streaming, gaming, socializing on social networking) in daily minutes. Socializing refers to time on social networking sites. Paired with individual-trait-level outcome: BMI z-score (physical health/biological measure qualifies as individual-trait)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: Î˛=-0.03 [-0.05, -0.002] for replacing 30 min socializing with 30 min physical activity (females); Î˛=-0.02 [-0.03, -0.01] for replacing 30 min streaming with 30 min sleep (males); CoDA estimates also provided

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results for screen timeâ†”BMI associations reported via isotemporal substitution and CoDA methods with 95% confidence intervals

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is primary IV predicting BMI as DV; not a longitudinal SM-as-DV-only design
notes: Phone/SM-specific variables (streaming, gaming, socializing) separately examined; longitudinal associations with individual-trait outcome; sex-stratified analyses
